world.name: ARI711S: Artificial Intelligence
world.description: Full exam prep for ARI711S — built from real test memos. Covers agents, search, CSPs, adversarial search, MDPs, and ML fundamentals.

concepts.name: What is an Agent?
concepts.definition: An entity that perceives its environment through sensors and acts upon it through actuators based on observations and goals.
concepts.analogy: A goalkeeper: perceives the ball's trajectory (sensors), decides where to dive (agent function), and dives (actuator).
breakdown: Percept — what the agent senses right now
Actuators — how the agent affects the world
Agent function — maps percept sequences to actions
Agent program — the implementation of that function
Autonomous agents act without human intervention

questions.prompt: What are the two main components of any agent?
questions.answer: Sensors (to perceive) and actuators (to act)

questions.prompt: What is the difference between an agent function and an agent program?
questions.answer: The agent function is the abstract mapping from percepts to actions; the agent program is its concrete implementation

questions.prompt: What makes an agent 'autonomous'?
questions.answer: It makes decisions and acts without human intervention

exam_block.text: Agents are defined by their ability to perceive and act. The agent function is the abstract specification of behaviour; the agent program runs on the physical architecture.

questions.prompt: Give one example of a real-world autonomous agent and identify its sensors and actuators.
questions.answer: Self-driving car — sensors: cameras, LIDAR, GPS; actuators: steering wheel, brakes, accelerator

concepts.name: Types of Agents
concepts.definition: Agents are classified by how much world knowledge they use to select actions.
concepts.analogy: From a simple light switch (reflex) to a chess grandmaster who plans 10 moves ahead (goal/utility).
breakdown: Simple Reflex — acts only on current percept, no memory
Model-Based Reflex — maintains internal state of the world
Goal-Based — considers future states to reach a goal
Utility-Based — picks the action that maximises a utility score
Learning Agent — improves its own behaviour over time

questions.prompt: Which agent type uses only the current percept and predefined condition-action rules?
questions.answer: Simple reflex agent

questions.prompt: Why does a model-based agent outperform a simple reflex agent in partially observable environments?
questions.answer: It keeps an internal model of the world so it can reason about parts of the environment it cannot currently perceive

questions.prompt: What is the key difference between a goal-based and a utility-based agent?
questions.answer: Goal-based checks if a goal is reached (binary); utility-based compares outcomes numerically and picks the best one

questions.prompt: An email spam filter that learns from flagged emails is what type of agent?
questions.answer: Learning agent

exam_block.text: EXAM FAVOURITE — Test 1 Q1 asked for a trade-off comparison between Simple Reflex and Model-Based agents in a vacuum cleaner. Know efficiency, adaptability, and robustness for each.

questions.prompt: Compare a simple reflex agent and a model-based agent across three factors: efficiency, adaptability, and robustness.
questions.answer: Efficiency: SRA is faster (no computation overhead); MBA is slower but makes better decisions. Adaptability: SRA cannot adapt to new situations without reprogramming; MBA adapts using its world model. Robustness: SRA fails in noisy/complex environments; MBA handles noise better by considering context and history.

questions.prompt: An autonomous vehicle must navigate safely — which agent type is most appropriate and why?
questions.answer: Utility-based agent — it must weigh multiple competing outcomes (speed, safety, fuel) and pick the action that maximises overall utility

concepts.name: Search Problem Components
concepts.definition: A formal problem defined by a state space, actions, start state, goal test, and solution.
concepts.analogy: A GPS route planner — the map is the state space, the destination is the goal, and the route is the solution.
breakdown: State space — all possible configurations of the world
Successor function — given a state, returns next states + action costs
Start state — where the agent begins
Goal test — function that checks if a state is the solution
Solution — sequence of actions from start to goal
Path cost — total cost of the solution path

questions.prompt: List the four key components of a search problem.
questions.answer: State space, successor function, start state, goal test

questions.prompt: What is the role of the successor function?
questions.answer: It defines which states can be reached from the current state and at what cost

questions.prompt: What is the difference between a search state and a world state?
questions.answer: A world state includes every detail of the environment; a search state keeps only the details needed for planning (abstraction)

questions.prompt: For a maze problem, what is the state space?
questions.answer: All (x, y) positions the agent can be in

exam_block.text: EXAM FAVOURITE — Test 1 Q2 asked to explain a search problem in your own words with all key components. Six marks = expect six distinct points.

questions.prompt: What is a solution in a search problem?
questions.answer: A sequence of actions (a plan) that transforms the start state into a goal state

concepts.name: Planning vs Identification Problems
concepts.definition: Planning problems care about the path to the goal; identification problems care only about reaching the goal state itself.
concepts.analogy: Planning = finding the route to a destination. Identification = just figuring out what city you're in.
breakdown: Planning: the PATH matters — costs and depth vary
Planning: heuristics guide towards the goal efficiently
Planning: environment is often dynamic (changes with actions)
Identification: the GOAL STATE matters, not how you got there
Identification: all paths are at the same depth (in some formulations)
Identification: environment is typically static

questions.prompt: In planning problems, what is the most important element — the path or the goal?
questions.answer: The path — reaching the goal efficiently matters

questions.prompt: In identification problems, what matters most?
questions.answer: The goal state itself — how you got there is irrelevant

questions.prompt: Give one example of a planning problem and one example of an identification problem.
questions.answer: Planning: navigating a robot through a maze (the route matters). Identification: diagnosing a disease from symptoms (we just need the correct diagnosis)

exam_block.text: EXAM FAVOURITE — Test 1 Q5 asked to explain the difference for 6 marks. Cover: path importance, cost/depth variation, heuristics, and environment type for each.

questions.prompt: Explain the difference between planning and identification problems across four dimensions.
questions.answer: Path importance: planning = path is key; identification = goal state is key. Cost/depth: planning = varied costs and depths; identification = all paths same depth. Heuristics: planning uses problem-specific heuristics; identification may not. Environment: planning = dynamic (actions change state); identification = static.

concepts.name: Algorithm Properties
concepts.definition: Four properties used to evaluate any search algorithm: completeness, optimality, time complexity, space complexity.
concepts.analogy: Judging a detective: Will they always find the culprit? Will they find the right one? How fast? How many notes do they take?
breakdown: Completeness — guaranteed to find a solution IF one exists
Optimality — guaranteed to find the LOWEST COST solution
Time Complexity — how many nodes are expanded (steps taken)
Space Complexity — how many nodes are held in memory at once

questions.prompt: What does 'complete' mean for a search algorithm?
questions.answer: It will always find a solution if one exists

questions.prompt: What does 'optimal' mean for a search algorithm?
questions.answer: It finds the solution with the lowest path cost

questions.prompt: Can an algorithm be complete but not optimal? Give an example.
questions.answer: Yes — BFS is complete (always finds a solution) but not always optimal (only optimal when costs are equal)

questions.prompt: What does space complexity measure?
questions.answer: How much memory the algorithm needs to run

exam_block.text: EXAM FAVOURITE — Test 1 Q3 gave descriptions and asked you to name the matching property. Know all four definitions cold.

questions.prompt: Match: 'A guarantee that the algorithm will find the solution with the lowest cost among all possible solutions.'
questions.answer: Optimality

questions.prompt: Match: 'A measure of how much memory the algorithm needs to run.'
questions.answer: Space Complexity

concepts.name: Breadth-First Search (BFS)
concepts.definition: Explores all nodes at the current depth before going deeper, using a FIFO queue.
concepts.analogy: Checking every room on Floor 1 before going to Floor 2.
breakdown: Data structure: Queue (FIFO)
Explores nodes level by level
Complete: Yes
Optimal: Yes — but ONLY when all step costs are equal
Time complexity: O(b^d) — b = branching factor, d = depth of shallowest solution
Space complexity: O(b^d) — must store all nodes at current level
Weakness: very high memory usage

questions.prompt: What data structure does BFS use?
questions.answer: Queue (FIFO — First In, First Out)

questions.prompt: Why is BFS optimal only when step costs are equal?
questions.answer: It finds the shallowest solution, which is only guaranteed to be the cheapest when all steps cost the same

questions.prompt: Why does BFS use more memory than DFS?
questions.answer: BFS stores every node at each level in the queue; the frontier grows exponentially

exam_block.text: EXAM FAVOURITE — Test 1 Q8 asked for the BFS expansion order on a tree. BFS always expands level by level, alphabetically when tied.

questions.prompt: For a tree rooted at A with children B,C; B has children D,E; C has children F,G — give BFS expansion order.
questions.answer: A, B, C, D, E, F, G

concepts.name: Depth-First Search (DFS)
concepts.definition: Explores as far as possible along each branch before backtracking, using a LIFO stack.
concepts.analogy: Following one hallway to its dead end before trying the next one.
breakdown: Data structure: Stack (LIFO — Last In, First Out)
Dives deep before backtracking
Complete: No — can get stuck in infinite loops (unless using graph search)
Optimal: No — may find a longer solution first
Time complexity: O(b^m) — m = maximum depth
Space complexity: O(bm) — only needs to store the current path
Strength: very memory efficient

questions.prompt: What data structure does DFS use?
questions.answer: Stack (LIFO)

questions.prompt: Why is DFS not optimal?
questions.answer: It may find a deep (expensive) solution before discovering a shorter one

questions.prompt: Compare DFS vs BFS on completeness.
questions.answer: BFS is complete. DFS is not complete in infinite state spaces — it can go down an infinite branch forever.

exam_block.text: EXAM FAVOURITE — Test 1 Q8. DFS expansion: follow the leftmost (alphabetically first) child all the way down before backtracking.

questions.prompt: For a tree rooted at A with children B,C; B has children D,E; C has children F,G — give DFS expansion order.
questions.answer: A, B, D, E, C, F, G (expands deepest first, alphabetical at each level)

concepts.name: Uniform Cost Search (UCS)
concepts.definition: Expands the node with the lowest cumulative path cost, using a priority queue ordered by g(n).
concepts.analogy: Always picking the cheapest flight leg first when planning a multi-stop trip.
breakdown: Data structure: Priority queue ordered by path cost g(n)
Always expands the cheapest unexplored node
Complete: Yes (if costs > 0)
Optimal: Yes — always finds the cheapest path
When all costs equal, behaves like BFS
Explores in 'rings' of increasing cost rather than depth

questions.prompt: What does UCS prioritise when choosing the next node to expand?
questions.answer: The node with the lowest total path cost so far — g(n)

questions.prompt: When does UCS behave exactly like BFS?
questions.answer: When all step costs are equal

questions.prompt: Why is UCS optimal but Greedy search is not?
questions.answer: UCS expands based on actual cost g(n); Greedy expands based on estimated remaining cost h(n) only, which can mislead it down expensive paths

exam_block.text: EXAM FAVOURITE — Test 1 Q8. UCS expansion order follows cost, not depth. Always expand the node with the smallest g(n) — break ties alphabetically.

questions.prompt: A→B costs 1, A→C costs 3, B→D costs 2, C→D costs 1. UCS expansion order to reach D?
questions.answer: A (g=0), B (g=1), D via B (g=3), then C (g=3) — D via B is found first with cost 3. Full order: A, B, C, D

concepts.name: Heuristics
concepts.definition: A function h(n) that estimates the cost from state n to the goal, used to guide informed search.
concepts.analogy: Estimating how far you are from the finish line — you use the straight-line distance, even though the actual road might be longer.
breakdown: h(n) = estimated cost from n to goal
Admissible heuristic: h(n) ≤ actual cost — never overestimates
Consistent heuristic: h(n) ≤ cost(n → n') + h(n') for every arc
Consistency implies admissibility (but not vice versa)
A consistent heuristic guarantees A* graph search finds optimal paths
Example: straight-line distance is admissible for road navigation

questions.prompt: What is an admissible heuristic?
questions.answer: One that never overestimates the true cost to reach the goal — h(n) ≤ actual cost

questions.prompt: What is a consistent (monotone) heuristic?
questions.answer: h(n) ≤ arc_cost(n → n') + h(n') for every successor n'. The estimated cost never decreases more than the arc cost.

questions.prompt: Why does A* graph search need consistency rather than just admissibility?
questions.answer: Graph search can close a node before finding the optimal path. Consistency ensures that once a node is expanded, the path to it is already optimal.

exam_block.text: EXAM FAVOURITE — Test 1 Q10. Always check: is h(n) ≤ actual cost? (admissibility). Then check the arc condition for consistency. An inconsistent heuristic can cause A* graph search to miss the optimal path.

questions.prompt: A has h=5, C has h=1. Arc A→C costs 3. Is this consistent?
questions.answer: Check: h(A) ≤ arc(A→C) + h(C) → 5 ≤ 3 + 1 = 4. FALSE — 5 > 4, so this heuristic is NOT consistent.

questions.prompt: If A* with a strict expanded list does NOT find the optimal path, what is the likely cause?
questions.answer: The heuristic is inconsistent. An inconsistent heuristic can cause a node to be closed with a suboptimal path, so the optimal route is never found.

concepts.name: Greedy Best-First Search
concepts.definition: Expands the node that appears closest to the goal based on h(n) alone.
concepts.analogy: Always taking the road that looks most directly towards your destination — ignoring how far you've already travelled.
breakdown: Priority queue ordered by h(n) only
Fast in practice — often reaches goal quickly
Not complete — can get stuck in loops
Not optimal — ignores actual path cost g(n)
Good first guess, not guaranteed best answer

questions.prompt: What value does Greedy search use to order the priority queue?
questions.answer: h(n) — the heuristic estimate to the goal only

questions.prompt: Why is Greedy search not optimal while A* is?
questions.answer: Greedy only uses h(n) and ignores the actual cost g(n); it can pick seemingly close nodes that require expensive paths

exam_block.text: EXAM FAVOURITE — Test 1 Q8. Greedy expands by h(n) — the node that looks closest to the goal, regardless of how you got there.

questions.prompt: Nodes: A(h=5), B(h=3), C(h=1), D(h=6). From A, you can reach B and C. Greedy expands next?
questions.answer: C — it has the lowest h(n) = 1

concepts.name: A* Search
concepts.definition: Expands the node with the lowest f(n) = g(n) + h(n), combining actual cost and heuristic estimate.
concepts.analogy: GPS that considers both how far you've driven AND how far you still need to go before choosing the next road.
breakdown: f(n) = g(n) + h(n)
g(n) = actual cost from start to n
h(n) = estimated cost from n to goal
Priority queue ordered by f(n)
Complete: Yes
Optimal: Yes — if heuristic is admissible (tree search) or consistent (graph search)
A* with strict expanded list: skips nodes already expanded
Optimality fails with inconsistent heuristic + strict expanded list

questions.prompt: What is the f(n) formula for A*?
questions.answer: f(n) = g(n) + h(n) — actual cost so far + heuristic estimate to goal

questions.prompt: When does A* terminate — when a goal is added to the frontier, or when it is expanded?
questions.answer: When a goal node is expanded (popped from the priority queue), not when it is first discovered

questions.prompt: A* step-by-step: Start=A (h=5). A→C costs 3, C has h=1. A→B costs 4, B has h=4. What is expanded first after A?
questions.answer: C — f(C) = g(3) + h(1) = 4. f(B) = g(4) + h(4) = 8. C has the lower f(n).

exam_block.text: EXAM FAVOURITE — Test 1 Q8 and Q9. Q9 required a full step-by-step A* table: path, path length, total f(n), and expanded list. Practise filling this table.

questions.prompt: Graph: A(h=5)→C(h=1) cost 3; A(h=5)→B(h=4) cost 1; B→C cost 1; C→H cost 2 (h=1); H→G cost 1 (h=0). Simulate A* with expanded list — give path, length, f(n), expanded list at each step.
questions.answer: Step 1: Expand A, f=5, expanded={A}. Step 2: Expand B (f=1+4=5) or C (f=3+1=4) — expand C first (lower f), f=4, expanded={A,C}. Step 3: Expand H (g=5, h=1, f=6), expanded={A,C,H}. Step 4: Expand G (g=6, h=0, f=6), expanded={A,C,H,G}. Path = A→C→H→G.

concepts.name: CSP Fundamentals
concepts.definition: A problem defined by variables, domains, and constraints — the goal is to assign values to all variables without violating any constraint.
concepts.analogy: Scheduling a school timetable: teachers (variables), time slots (domains), 'same teacher can't teach two classes simultaneously' (constraints).
breakdown: Variables — the things that need values
Domains — the set of valid values for each variable
Constraints — rules that restrict which value combinations are valid
Solution — a complete assignment that satisfies all constraints
Constraint graph — nodes = variables, edges = constraints between pairs

questions.prompt: What are the three key components of a CSP?
questions.answer: Variables, Domains, Constraints

questions.prompt: What does a constraint graph represent?
questions.answer: Nodes are variables; edges connect pairs of variables that share a constraint

questions.prompt: In a map-colouring problem with 3 colours and 4 regions, what are the variables and domains?
questions.answer: Variables = each region; Domains = {Red, Green, Blue} for each variable

exam_block.text: EXAM FAVOURITE — Supp Test Q4. Given a scheduling scenario, you must: (1) draw the constraint graph, (2) list variables and domains, (3) run backtracking. Know all three steps.

questions.prompt: Lecturers T, L, B, C, S, P, N must be assigned time slots 1–4. T can only be assigned slot 1. Course VIII students want to see B, C, and N together (same slot). What constraint does this create?
questions.answer: B, C, and N must all share the same time slot. This means B-C, B-N, and C-N are all constrained to be equal (same slot)

concepts.name: Backtracking Search
concepts.definition: Assigns one variable at a time and backtracks when a dead end is reached — no value satisfies the constraints.
concepts.analogy: Filling in a Sudoku: if you get stuck, erase the last number you wrote and try the next possibility.
breakdown: Assign variables one at a time in order
At each step, check constraint consistency
If no valid value exists for a variable — backtrack
Naive backtracking does NOT look ahead
More efficient than generating all assignments then checking

questions.prompt: When does backtracking trigger?
questions.answer: When no value in a variable's domain satisfies the current constraints

questions.prompt: What is the key limitation of plain backtracking without filtering?
questions.answer: It only detects failure after a full dead end — it doesn't look ahead to prevent wasted assignments

exam_block.text: EXAM FAVOURITE — Test 1 Q11, Supp Q4. You will be asked to trace backtracking step by step, showing assignments and domain changes. Know how to draw the search tree with backtrack marks.

questions.prompt: Variables 1,2,3 each with domain {R,G,B}. Constraint: adjacent nodes must differ. Assignments in order: 1=R, then try 2=R — valid? Then 2=G — valid?
questions.answer: 2=R — invalid if 2 is adjacent to 1 (same colour). 2=G — valid if no other constraint violated. Continue with 3.

concepts.name: Filtering Techniques
concepts.definition: Methods that reduce variable domains DURING search to detect dead ends early, before fully committing to an assignment.
concepts.analogy: Before placing a chess piece, check if it will immediately cause a problem — don't wait until you're checkmated.
breakdown: Forward Checking — after assigning a variable, prune inconsistent values from neighbours' domains
If any neighbour's domain becomes empty → backtrack immediately
Arc Consistency (AC-3) — ensures every value in a domain has a valid counterpart in connected variables
Arc consistency is stronger than forward checking
Constraint Propagation — propagates arc consistency repeatedly throughout the CSP

questions.prompt: What does forward checking do after a variable is assigned?
questions.answer: It checks all neighbouring variables and removes values that are inconsistent with the new assignment

questions.prompt: What triggers immediate backtracking in forward checking?
questions.answer: When any unassigned variable's domain becomes empty

questions.prompt: How is arc consistency stronger than forward checking?
questions.answer: Forward checking only checks neighbours of the most recently assigned variable; arc consistency ensures all variable pairs are mutually consistent throughout the whole CSP

exam_block.text: EXAM FAVOURITE — Test 1 Q6 (6 marks) and Q11. Q6: name and explain 3 filtering techniques. Q11: trace forward checking step by step, showing domain state after each assignment.

questions.prompt: Assign 1=R. Variables 2,3,4,5 have domains {R,G,B}. 1-2 and 1-3 are constrained. After forward checking on assignment 1=R, what are the updated domains?
questions.answer: Domain of 2 and 3: R removed → {G, B}. Domains of 4, 5 (not adjacent to 1): unchanged → {R,G,B}

concepts.name: Minimax Algorithm
concepts.definition: A recursive algorithm that determines the optimal move for a player in a two-player zero-sum game by assuming the opponent plays optimally.
concepts.analogy: You try to maximise your score while your opponent tries to minimise it — both playing perfectly.
breakdown: MAX player — tries to maximise the value
MIN player — tries to minimise the value
Terminal states have utility values
Minimax value = backed-up utility assuming both play optimally
Complete: Yes (if tree is finite)
Optimal: Yes — against a perfect opponent
Time: O(b^m) — impractical for large games like chess

questions.prompt: What does the MAX player do in minimax?
questions.answer: Picks the action that maximises the utility value

questions.prompt: Why is minimax optimal only against a perfect opponent?
questions.answer: It assumes the opponent always makes the best possible move; a weak opponent might allow better outcomes than minimax predicts

questions.prompt: Terminal values at leaves: [3, 5, 2, 9, 1, 7]. Tree depth 2 — what is the minimax value at the root (MAX node with two MIN children)?
questions.answer: Left MIN child = min(3,5,2) = 2. Right MIN child = min(9,1,7) = 1. Root MAX = max(2,1) = 2.

exam_block.text: Know how to back up minimax values through a tree. Practice tracing leaf values upward — alternating MIN and MAX layers.

questions.prompt: In a zero-sum game, if MAX gains 5 points, how many points does MIN gain?
questions.answer: MIN loses 5 (gains -5) — the gains always sum to zero

concepts.name: Alpha-Beta Pruning
concepts.definition: An optimisation of minimax that prunes branches that cannot influence the final decision, reducing the search tree size.
concepts.analogy: You don't need to check every single option if you already know a better one exists — just cut the dead branches.
breakdown: Alpha (α) — best value MAX can guarantee so far
Beta (β) — best value MIN can guarantee so far
Prune when α ≥ β — current branch can't improve the result
Does NOT change the final minimax value
Best case: O(b^(m/2)) — doubles effective search depth
Effective only in two-player zero-sum games

questions.prompt: What does alpha represent in alpha-beta pruning?
questions.answer: The best value MAX can guarantee from the current position or above

questions.prompt: Under what condition is a branch pruned?
questions.answer: When α ≥ β — MAX already has a better option, so MIN's subtree is irrelevant

questions.prompt: Does alpha-beta pruning change the final result compared to plain minimax?
questions.answer: No — it always produces the same result as minimax, just more efficiently

exam_block.text: EXAM FAVOURITE — Supp Test Q1 (T/F). Alpha-beta CAN improve minimax efficiency in two-player zero-sum games — TRUE. Know: it doesn't change the outcome, it just skips irrelevant branches.

questions.prompt: Alpha-beta pruning is most effective when nodes are explored in what order?
questions.answer: Best moves first — if the best moves are explored early, more branches can be pruned

concepts.name: MDP Components
concepts.definition: A mathematical framework for modelling decision-making in stochastic (probabilistic) environments.
concepts.analogy: A robot in a grid: it chooses actions but can slip — the outcome is probabilistic, not deterministic.
breakdown: State (S) — complete description of the agent's situation
Action (A) — decisions the agent can make
Transition T(s, a, s') — probability of reaching s' from s via action a
Reward R(s, a, s') — numerical feedback for taking action a from s
Policy π — mapping from states to actions (what to do in each state)
Discount factor γ — how much future rewards are discounted (0 ≤ γ ≤ 1)

questions.prompt: What are the five components of an MDP?
questions.answer: State, Action, Transition function, Reward function, Policy

questions.prompt: What does the discount factor γ control?
questions.answer: How much the agent values future rewards compared to immediate rewards. γ close to 0 = myopic; γ close to 1 = far-sighted

questions.prompt: What is a policy in an MDP?
questions.answer: A mapping from every state to an action — it tells the agent what to do in any situation

exam_block.text: EXAM FAVOURITE — Supp Test Q2 (4 marks) and Q5 (10 marks). Q2: define all MDP components. Q5: apply Value Iteration using the Bellman equation.

questions.prompt: What does the transition function T(s, a, s') represent?
questions.answer: The probability of transitioning to state s' when action a is taken in state s

concepts.name: Value Iteration
concepts.definition: An algorithm that computes the optimal value function by repeatedly applying the Bellman equation until convergence.
concepts.analogy: Estimating the value of every square on a board game by updating each square based on its neighbours, over and over until nothing changes.
breakdown: Initialise all state values to 0: V⁰(s) = 0
Bellman equation: V*(s) = max_a Σ T(s,a,s')[R(s,a,s') + γV*(s')]
Each iteration: V^(k+1)(s) = max_a Σ T(s,a,s')[R(s,a,s') + γV^k(s')]
Repeat until values converge (change < small epsilon)
The optimal policy selects the action that achieves V*(s) at each state

questions.prompt: What is the initial value of all states in value iteration?
questions.answer: 0 — V⁰(s) = 0 for all states

questions.prompt: Write out the Bellman equation used in value iteration.
questions.answer: V*(s) = max_a Σ_{s'} T(s, a, s') [R(s, a, s') + γ V*(s')]

questions.prompt: State S1: two actions. Action 1 leads to S2 (r=3) with prob 1.0. Action 2 leads to S3 (r=10) with prob 0.5 and S0 (r=1) with prob 0.5. γ=0.9. V⁰=0. Compute J¹(S1).
questions.answer: Action 1: 1.0 × (3 + 0.9×0) = 3. Action 2: 0.5×(10 + 0) + 0.5×(1 + 0) = 5 + 0.5 = 5.5. J¹(S1) = max(3, 5.5) = 5.5. (Exact values depend on graph — check with your specific MDP diagram.)

exam_block.text: EXAM FAVOURITE — Supp Test Q5. Given a specific MDP diagram, compute J¹(S1) and J²(S1). Use the Bellman equation. Remember γ=0.9 in the test. Expected answers: J¹=2, J²=7.4 for that specific graph.

questions.prompt: Why is the discount factor γ important in value iteration?
questions.answer: It prevents infinite sums in infinite-horizon problems and controls how much the agent prioritises future vs immediate rewards

concepts.name: Learning Paradigms
concepts.definition: Machine learning is categorised by the type of data and feedback used: supervised, unsupervised, and reinforcement learning.
concepts.analogy: Supervised = learning maths with an answer key. Unsupervised = sorting LEGO by shape with no instructions. Reinforcement = learning to ride a bike through trial and error.
breakdown: Supervised Learning — uses labelled training data; learns input-output mapping
Goal of supervised: prediction (classification or regression)
Examples: decision trees, neural networks, SVM, linear regression
Unsupervised Learning — no labels; finds hidden patterns in data
Goal of unsupervised: discover structure (clustering, dimensionality reduction)
Examples: k-means clustering, PCA
Reinforcement Learning — agent learns by receiving rewards/penalties from environment
Goal of RL: learn optimal policy through trial and error
Examples: game-playing AI (AlphaGo), robot locomotion

questions.prompt: What type of data does supervised learning require?
questions.answer: Labelled data — inputs paired with correct output labels

questions.prompt: What is the goal of unsupervised learning?
questions.answer: To discover hidden patterns or structure in unlabelled data

questions.prompt: What is the key difference in learning feedback between supervised and reinforcement learning?
questions.answer: Supervised uses explicit correct labels provided upfront; reinforcement learning receives only reward/penalty signals after taking actions

questions.prompt: An AI that learns to play chess by playing against itself and winning/losing — which paradigm?
questions.answer: Reinforcement learning — it learns through trial and error with rewards (win) and penalties (lose)

exam_block.text: EXAM FAVOURITE — Supp Test Q3 (6 marks). Two sub-questions: (1) supervision type, (2) learning goal. Know all three paradigms across both dimensions.

questions.prompt: Compare supervised, unsupervised, and reinforcement learning on two dimensions: type of data used, and learning goal.
questions.answer: Supervised: labelled data → prediction. Unsupervised: unlabelled data → pattern discovery. Reinforcement: reward signals → optimal decision-making policy.
